data.title: I'm exactly 5 years from retirement. Here's what I'll do first to prepare.
data.description:
By Beth Pinsker

I just turned 541/2, and this could be my most consequential age milestone, because it's the ideal time for me to start detailed planning for my golden years

I'm ready to declare my retirement date five years from now.

I stopped celebrating half-birthdays decades ago, and today is No. 541/2 - a significant milestone because I've decided to retire in exactly five years. I always tell people that when they hit this moment there's important work to do. Now it's my turn to practice what I preach.

The first order of business: declaring my intentions.

This is important because retirement sneaks up on most people. A good quarter of adults in the U.S. today think they'll never retire - but then, at the same time, half of older workers report being forced out of their jobs earlier than they had planned. The demographic reality today is that the average retirement age is 65 for men and 63 for women.

Leaving full-time work before 60 means I'll be on the early side, but I picked the very specific age of 591/2 because that's when I can access the funds in my retirement accounts without penalty. For me, the stars align at that particular time for a number of other reasons as well, including my kids finishing college, my partner's pension vesting and even anticipating my aging dog's eventual need to move to a place with no stairs.

Most people today work until they are older: Only 11% of people ages 55 to 59 are retired, according to Gallup. That number nearly triples in the next older cohort of 60 to 64, and jumps to nearly 70% by the ages of 65 to 69.

Those ages keep trending higher, both because of people living longer and because Americans are not saving enough. A Fidelity study shows the average 401(k) balance of those age 55 to 59 is $245,000. Now, that doesn't include other savings and home value, but it's still likely the core amount most of the people in that age range will use to live on in retirement. If that doesn't seem like enough, the easy answer is to work a few more years.

I could stick it out past 60 - if my job would have me, which is not guaranteed in the media - but I'm more concerned with my own longevity than I am with running out of money. My parents both died when they were 76, five years apart. I'm healthier than they were, but I can't fathom a scenario where I'd work at full steam until 70 and then only have six years of freedom to explore what else there is out there for me. Writers never really fully stop - just ask my "Unretirement" colleague Richard Eisenberg - but we can walk away from daily deadlines and limited vacation time.

To make it all work, of course, I need to do more than just share my goal with the universe (and MarketWatch readers). I've got to make sure that I'm doing the right things financially to set myself up for the future.

The reason that being five years out from retirement is such a big deal is that it gives me time to plan. Any further away than that, and the details get too fuzzy; any closer, and you don't have a lot of room for adjustments. Your age doesn't matter here - you could be 541/2 like me, or 10 years older or younger. What's important is giving yourself enough runway.

So my next step was to talk to some experts to start making my plan for how it's best to save and invest.

Emily Guy Birken, author of "The Five Years Before You Retire," told me that getting started was very simple. "The plan is knowing what you have and what you need, and then figuring out how to bridge the gap between those two things," she said.

What you have is your income. If you stop working before you claim Social Security, you might have no money at all coming in at first. This is what scares people the most about stopping work before at least age 62. It's also what drives many to claim at the earliest possible age, permanently reducing their monthly benefit.

What you need is your core budget of necessities: housing, utilities, health insurance, food and transportation. Then you can figure in your discretionary spending for vacations and other luxuries. Birken said it helps to track your spending in the early part of the five years.

"Get a baseline," she said. "You'll be ahead of many people, because then you can figure out if there's a difference between what you're bringing in and what you're spending."

She told me this is not the time to worry about the 60/40 balancing of my investment portfolio or whether I should do Roth conversions. Instead, I should be focusing on the broader picture first. What do I want retirement to look like? What will I do? Where do I want to live? Then I can figure out what all that will potentially cost.

Filling the gap

If you will be bringing in less than you will be spending when you retire, you need to make some changes. That usually requires earning more, spending less or pulling from savings in a structured way.

Jeanne Thompson had to do all of the above when she retired earlier than she had originally planned. Thompson, formerly a retirement expert at Fidelity, took a buyout when she was 54 and is now hitting five years into retirement. She didn't take the time to plan, as she had counseled other people, because she had to make a decision about the offer in front of her and, like me, was thinking about her mother, who died at 72.

"I do regret it," she said of her buyout and her lack of preparation for life after work. "I was disoriented at first. It's hard to step out of that go-go mentality."

Her retirement also coincided with the 2022 market downturn. "It was a harrowing experience," she said. "This is what everyone warns about: You retire, and the market tanks."

The first year, she and her husband, who was already retired, didn't spend much at all. "We had travel plans, but were like little squirrels because you don't know how long the bear market will last," Thompson said. When the market bounced back in 2023, they roamed the globe. But then work called again, at least part time, and Thompson is now a senior retirement consultant for LPL Financial.

Planning for curveballs

There are other issues that can jam you up in early retirement that you can plan for in the preretirement years. Thompson said her biggest concern became health coverage. If you are under the age of 65 and do not have retiree health benefits from an employer, insurance can be a major expense. This is something you can price out and save for while you are still working.

"You can decide to wait until 63 and you can do Cobra until you can get Medicare," suggested Thompson. Or you can coordinate with a spouse so that one person is working to provide coverage. Thompson also wishes she had contributed earlier to a health savings account so that she could pull out tax-free money for health expenses once she stopped working.

Another potential hitch could come from family members - especially if you are in the sandwich generation, between children and aging parents who might need financial assistance.

One key reason I waited until now to consider myself in the five-year zone before retirement is because I needed my younger child, now a high-school senior, to pick a college so I'd know how much I'd be paying in tuition for the next four years. That's basically a fixed cost for me, but the amount of help my two kids might need after they graduate college is controllable.

"Your kids will cost you as long as you let them cost you," said Thompson. I plan to follow her lead after they graduate - which was to sit her kids down with a spreadsheet and explain what they could afford on their own salaries, and then send them off into the world to live within their means.

I have to take that same advice for myself when it comes to managing my savings. As financial educator Mari Adam told me: "My experience is that overspending is always the most dangerous. If money goes out, it's out."

There will still be unknowns

You can't know everything that will happen, but thinking through as much as you can will forestall a lot of bad outcomes. Your plan does not have to be a 30-page document filled with charts and market-return projections.

Mari Adam and Jeanne Thompson both use spreadsheets to keep track of their net worths and spending. Birken said you can synthesize the information you need down to a few lines that articulate your steps for making up the difference between what you have and what you spend.

"Agility is great, but, unless you have a plan, you're still flailing around," Birken said. She's heard people say that you can't have a contingency plan for everything - "but yes, yes you can," she emphasized. Birken often describes learning about money as like learning to cook, but safer: "If you are attempting a chicken dish, there is a nonzero chance you can kill somebody, but nobody is terrified of that. If you screw up with money, nobody will die. So lower the terror factor."

I'm taking this to heart and going into these next five years with my eyes open to all the possibilities - both good and bad.

Want to tell me about your retirement plan or give me advice on mine? You can write to me at beth.pinsker@marketwatch.com. Please put "Fix My Portfolio" in the subject line.

You can also join the Retirement conversation in our Facebook community: Retire Better with MarketWatch.

By submitting your story to Dow Jones & Co., the publisher of MarketWatch, you understand and agree that we may use your story, or versions of it, in all media and platforms, including via third parties.

More Fix My Portfolio

I checked out an Advanced Placement personal-finance class. Here's what I learned.

Nearly 90% of American adults are vulnerable because they are missing this key document

You only really need to know one thing to save for retirement

-Beth Pinsker

This content was created by MarketWatch, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. MarketWatch is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal. 




  (END) Dow Jones Newswires

data.title: Copperline Golf Launches AI Voice Caddy App on iOS and Android
data.description:
Copperline Golf Launches AI Voice Caddy App on iOS and Android

Copperline Golf Launches AI Voice Caddy App on iOS and Android

PR Newswire

SCOTTSDALE, Ariz., April 23, 2026

Three voice personalities turn any phone into a real-time caddy.

SCOTTSDALE, Ariz., April 23, 2026 /PRNewswire/ -- Copperline Golf today announced that its AI-powered caddy app is now available on the Apple App Store and Google Play. The launch marks the first consumer golf app to ship with three distinct AI caddy personalities, letting players choose the coaching style that fits their round.

Unlike traditional golf GPS apps that show yardages and leave the decision to the player, Copperline Golf acts as a true on-course caddy: it listens, asks follow-ups, factors in wind, elevation, lie, and hazards, and delivers a club recommendation out loud — the way a tour caddy would.

Three Voice Personalities, One Caddy

Golfers tap a single button to start a conversation with one of three personalities:

  • Talk It Through — a full-conversation caddy that walks through the shot and talks strategy.
  • Just The Facts — a no-nonsense caddy that delivers club, carry, and aim in one clean callout.
  • Minimal — one sentence, one club, done. Built for tournaments and quiet courses.

All three run on the same engine: a personalized shot-dispersion model, real-time weather and elevation data, GPS course mapping with vector overlays, and a calculation engine refined over hundreds of rounds of testing.

Why Voice Changes the Round

Copperline Golf replaces the phone-out, tap-tap-tap model with a conversation. Players keep the phone pocketed, use Bluetooth earbuds, and get a recommendation before reaching the ball. For the 40+ million golfers in North America who play without a human caddy, the promise is simple: better decisions, fewer blow-ups, more enjoyable rounds.

What's Included at Launch

  • Three AI voice caddy personalities
  • Personalized club recommendations based on your shot dispersion
  • GPS hole maps with satellite imagery and vector overlays
  • Automatic wind, temperature, elevation, and lie detection
  • Passive shot tracking with round-level analytics
  • Bluetooth hands-free support
  • Five-tier skill system: Beginner through Tour Pro

Availability

Copperline Golf is a free download today, with an optional Pro subscription for unlimited voice sessions and advanced analytics.

About Copperline Golf

Copperline Golf is building the first AI-native golf experience — a caddy that learns your game, understands the course, and talks you through every shot.

copperlinegolf.com

View original content to download multimedia:https://www.prnewswire.com/news-releases/copperline-golf-launches-ai-voice-caddy-app-on-ios-and-android-302751990.html

SOURCE Copperline Golf

Brent crude futures settled above $100 a barrel on Wednesday as tensions in the Strait of Hormuz mounted.

The U.S., France, Japan, India and the U.K. recorded a pickup in factory output, but Germany is experiencing a sharp decline in services activity and a slowdown in manufacturing.

The number of people who filed for unemployment benefits was 214,000 in the week through April 18, higher than the 208,000 reported a week earlier, the Labor Department said.

Trump Administration Reclassifies Marijuana as Less Dangerous Drug

The policy shift could make it much easier to buy and sell pot and reward investors in the multibillion-dollar cannabis industry.

data.title: Yorktowne Cabinetry Reveals Reinvigorated Brand Identity to Reinforce Commitment to Luxurious Everyday Living
data.description:
Yorktowne Cabinetry Reveals Reinvigorated Brand Identity to Reinforce Commitment to Luxurious Everyday Living

Yorktowne Cabinetry, a leader in expertly crafted cabinetry that brings high standards and enduring beauty to homes across the country and part of the Cabinetworks Group portfolio, recently revealed an updated brand identity, marking a modern evolution of one of the most steadfast names in semi-custom cabinetry.

The refreshed brand identity introduces a new logo and refined visual voice that portrays a greater understanding of what Yorktowne means to those who work with the brand and those who live with it, as well as a sharpened brand promise anchored by a new tagline: Live-in Luxury. The new tagline signifies a promise built on more than 100 years of craftsmanship and the belief that true luxury isn’t something homeowners should admire from afar, but something they can experience and enjoy every day.

“Beauty and quality have always been essential to Yorktowne,” said Kristen Phipps, director of brand and product for Cabinetworks Group. “This refresh provides a visual identity that aligns with what our dealers and designers have always known, solidifying that Yorktowne is a name worth investing in, for themselves and for their clients.”

At the heart of the brand evolution is a refined focus on three core brand pillars: everyday ease, personalized style and legacy standards. Yorktowne Cabinetry serves as the foundation for everyday comfort, blending thoughtful design and flexible function for a truly inviting experience. The brand offers an abundance of style options to ensure true personalization, turning each room into a reflection of individual vision. These updates remain deeply rooted in the company's Legacy Standards, honoring over a century of artisanal craftsmanship that delivers enduring beauty and lasting comfort.

“Yorktowne design partners thrive on connection, cultivating passionate relationships with their clients,” said Phipps. “The thoughtful details they integrate into each room tell a story, as our designers marry Yorktowne’s intrinsic quality and enduring beauty with a client’s personal style to craft a living legacy for every home.”

The new logo, colors and refined positioning can now be seen across all mediums.

To learn more about Yorktowne Cabinetry and explore the new offerings, visit YorktowneCabinetry.com.

Kate De Michieli
Kate@oneeightohpr.com
941-706-6236

data.title: Orion Strategies Continues Expansion in Columbus, Ohio Office
data.description:
Orion Strategies Continues Expansion in Columbus, Ohio Office

Orion Strategies, a nationally recognized strategic communications firm specializing in energy, large industrial projects, and infrastructure, has announced the expansion of its Columbus, Ohio office with the addition of Prescott Carey, Senior Manager, and Tyler Harmon, Manager. Carey and Harmon will support the firm’s growing project portfolio across the Midwest including Ohio, bringing with them significant experience in state government and public affairs.

Carey most recently served as District Director for Congressman Mike Rulli (OH-6) where he led district operations, oversaw district services, and coordinated stakeholder engagement across Ohio’s 6th Congressional District. Carey managed federal casework and advised on the regional impact of energy and commerce policy, while building partnerships with public, private, and nonprofit leaders to advance energy, infrastructure, and economic development initiatives throughout underserved areas.

Harmon previously served for five years as a Legislative Aide in the Ohio House of Representatives for State Representative Jean Schmidt, where he worked as Clerk for the House Health and Families and Aging Committee and championed the development and advancement of legislation. He worked closely with stakeholders, policy advisors, and caucus leadership to shape policy initiatives across multiple General Assemblies. Harmon also drafted legislative materials and briefings for Schmidt, while overseeing office operations, constituent services, and junior staff.

Their combined expertise in legislative processes, stakeholder engagement, and Ohio’s political landscape will further strengthen Orion’s ability to deliver strategic counsel and execute successful energy and infrastructure initiatives across the U.S.

“Orion Strategies is continuing to build on its momentum, and we are excited to expand our Columbus office with the addition of Prescott and Tyler,” said Curtis Wilkerson, CEO of Orion Strategies. “As our portfolio grows rapidly, it is essential that we bring on individuals with deep experience and backgrounds in Ohio state politics. Prescott and Tyler each bring valuable insight, relationships, and strategic expertise that will strengthen our team and support our clients across the region.”

Orion Strategies is a strategic communication firm that was formed 19 years ago and has built a national reputation advising energy projects across the country. The firm presently has projects in 20 states.

Matthew Hammond, Orion Strategies
mhammond@orion-strategies.com

View source version on businesswire.com: https://www.businesswire.com/news/home/20260423171111/en/

data.title: I'm 56. My home has $400,000 in equity. If I lose my job, should I do a reverse mortgage?
data.description:
By Quentin Fottrell

'I currently make $145,000, and my wife makes $98,000'

"I might also consider a part-time job at Home Depot." (Photo subject is a model.)

Dear Quentin,

I'm 56, with $1,700 monthly mortgage payments ($400,000 in equity), regular utility bills, $550,000 in a 401(k), and $80,000 in savings, along with a $2,300 per month tax-free VA disability payment. I currently make $145,000 and my wife makes $98,000.

If my job is eliminated next year, would it make sense to do a reverse mortgage and take the $400,000 in equity, my VA disability and upcoming Social Security? I'm wondering if that, combined with my disability income and no house payment, would allow me to retire next year.

I might also consider a part-time job at Home Depot. Men in my family tend to die young (late 60s), so I'm worried about my remaining years.

Husband and Homeowner

Don't miss: 'I plan to exit corporate life': I'm 50 and have $400,000. My wife is a teacher. Can I retire at 55?

You can email The Moneyist with any financial and ethical questions at qfottrell@marketwatch.com. The Moneyist regrets he cannot reply to questions individually.

An ideal candidate is typically a homeowner 62 or older with significant home equity - 50% or even more - and plans to remain in their primary home in the long run.

Dear Homeowner,

Let's break out an acronym before we break out your home equity.

You have not lost your job. It's one of those events that could happen. And, while it's always good to have an emergency fund and to be prepared for the worst while living your best life, I wonder if the base note in your letter - like a perfume - is FEAR. And we all know some of the many acronyms for that. "False evidence appearing real" is one of them.

Now that's out of the way, we can answer your question. It's too early. To qualify for most types of reverse mortgages or a home-equity conversion mortgage, you must be at least 62 years old for government-backed reverse mortgages, although some private reverse-mortgage firms may be available to homeowners in your age group. That's for starters.

You have a $550,000 401(k), $80,000 in cash savings, and, importantly, your wife earns $98,000 a year. That could potentially sustain a household. That, plus your $2,300 a month in VA disability benefits, pays your mortgage and leaves you with likely enough left over for insurance, taxes, utilities and food. But does it pay for leisure? Over to you.

You reduce your Social Security benefits by 30% if you claim at 62 and you forgo a roughly 8%-a-year additional increase if you retire at 67, your full retirement age, rather than 70. Taking 4% withdrawal on your $550,000 gives you about $1,833 a month. Based on the stock market's historical averages, that should last you 30 years - but there are no guarantees.

The expense of reverse mortgages

Earlier this month, New View Advisors released a report claiming that private reverse mortgages now make up just over half (52%) of the market, up from 30% at the end of 2024, outpacing government-backed reverse mortgages for the first time. From January to March, private reverse mortgage lending hit $953 million, up from $470 million a year earlier.

But reverse mortgages don't come cheap. I'm assuming you don't have children. With a reverse mortgage, the interest is deferred and added to the value of the loan. Interest is only charged on the amount you receive from the bank. You must include maintenance fees, home and mortgage insurance and property taxes in your budget as ongoing housing costs.

An ideal candidate is typically a homeowner 62 or older with significant home equity - 50% or even more - and plans to remain in their primary home long-term. You should be financially stable enough to pay property taxes, homeowners' insurance, and maintenance costs. Failure to do so could result in foreclosure (as the warnings often say).

It pays to wait. "The older you are, the better," says Longbridge Financial. "When it comes to calculating the amount of proceeds you can receive with a reverse mortgage, known as the 'principal limit,' your age is a key factor. Since the principal limit accounts for the estimated length of the loan, the older your age, the more cash you'll be able to receive."

"If you plan to have a spouse or co-borrower included on your reverse mortgage loan, the age of the youngest borrower will be used to determine the principal limit," the Paramus, N.J. reverse-mortgage specialist adds. "Similar to collecting Social Security benefits, the longer you can defer tapping into your home equity, the more money you can ultimately receive."

Restricting vs. expanding your freedom

With a reverse mortgage, you are not gaining the kind of financial freedom many people seek. Moving triggers immediate repayment. Reverse mortgages require that the property on which the mortgage is held be your primary residence. So what happens if you and your wife want to downsize, move abroad or move into assisted living?

Reverse-mortgage rates tend to be higher than those for home-equity loans or lines of credit, according to AARP. "If you die, your heirs will need to pay off the loan's balance. They'll have six months to satisfy the debt, though they may be able to get a 90-day extension. Heirs can choose to either sell the property or purchase it for 95% of its appraised value."

The lender pays you money and uses your house as collateral. The income is not taxable, and it won't affect your Social Security or Medicare benefits. Because you're borrowing money against the equity in your house, you don't have monthly loan payments. You can, if you choose, pay back the reverse mortgage in a lump sum - if you suddenly were to come into money.

Reverse mortgages require you to keep up with property taxes, insurance, maintenance and live in the home as your primary residence to avoid foreclosure. Additionally, AARP highlights that scams are common in the industry, and the CFPB has taken action against several lenders for issues such as poor communication, misleading advertising and preventable foreclosures.

Don't pull the emergency brakes on your financial life quite yet.
